explain.rule: Relative clauses give essential information about a noun — they tell us WHICH one. They use relative pronouns: who (for people), which (for things), that (for people or things), where (for places), when (for times), whose (for possession). Defining relative clauses do not use commas — the information is essential to identify which person or thing we mean. If you remove a defining relative clause, the sentence loses its essential meaning.
explain.why_matters: Relative clauses let you build more complex, natural sentences and give precise information without starting a new sentence. They appear in all forms of English and are essential at B1 level.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew uses ש (she) for all relative clauses regardless of the noun type: הגבר שאני מכיר (the man that I know), הבית שהוא גר בו (the house that he lives in). In English you must choose who/which/that/where/when depending on what the noun is. Also: in Hebrew you often need a pronoun at the end (גר בו), but in English relative clauses, the pronoun is dropped: 'the house that he lives in' — NOT 'the house that he lives in it'.
explain.tip: Quick guide: WHO = person (the man who called), WHICH = thing (the book which I read), THAT = person or thing (informal), WHERE = place (the city where I grew up), WHEN = time (the day when we met), WHOSE = possession (the woman whose car was stolen). Object relative pronouns (when the relative pronoun is the object) can be omitted in informal English: 'The book (that) I read' — the 'that' is optional here.

relative_pronouns.example: The woman who lives next door is a doctor.
The book which I borrowed is brilliant.

relative_pronouns.use: subject or object — people or things (informal)
relative_pronouns.example: The car that I bought last year broke down.
The city where I grew up has changed a lot.
I'll never forget the day when we met.
The man whose wallet was stolen called the police.

good_examples.sentence: The woman who called you is my manager.
good_examples.audio_text: The woman who called you is my manager.
good_examples.note: who = subject of the relative clause (she called)

good_examples.sentence: I lost the keys which you gave me.
good_examples.audio_text: I lost the keys which you gave me.
good_examples.note: which = object (you gave them to me)

good_examples.sentence: This is the restaurant that I told you about.
good_examples.audio_text: This is the restaurant that I told you about.

good_examples.sentence: The city where I grew up is much smaller than Tel Aviv.
good_examples.audio_text: The city where I grew up is much smaller than Tel Aviv.

good_examples.sentence: I still remember the day when we first met.
good_examples.audio_text: I still remember the day when we first met.

good_examples.sentence: The woman whose car was stolen reported it to the police.
good_examples.audio_text: The woman whose car was stolen reported it to the police.

good_examples.sentence: The people who work here are very dedicated.
good_examples.audio_text: The people who work here are very dedicated.

good_examples.sentence: Is this the book you were talking about?
good_examples.audio_text: Is this the book you were talking about?

good_examples.sentence: The company that made this phone went bankrupt.
good_examples.audio_text: The company that made this phone went bankrupt.

good_examples.sentence: I need someone who can speak Arabic.
good_examples.audio_text: I need someone who can speak Arabic.
good_examples.note: who for a person — subject position

bad_examples.wrong: The man which I spoke to was very helpful.
bad_examples.correct: The man who I spoke to was very helpful.
bad_examples.reason: which is for things. For people use who (or that in informal English).

bad_examples.wrong: The book who I read was excellent.
bad_examples.correct: The book which/that I read was excellent.
bad_examples.reason: who is for people only. For things use which or that.

bad_examples.wrong: The city where I grew up it is beautiful.
bad_examples.correct: The city where I grew up is beautiful.
bad_examples.reason: Don't repeat the subject with a pronoun after a relative clause. 'where I grew up' already refers to the city — don't add 'it'.

bad_examples.wrong: She is the woman whose she lost her passport.
bad_examples.correct: She is the woman who lost her passport.
bad_examples.reason: whose = possession (the woman's passport). If it's her own action use who: the woman who lost her passport.

bad_examples.wrong: This is the hotel that we stayed in it.
bad_examples.correct: This is the hotel that we stayed in.
bad_examples.reason: Don't add a pronoun (it/him/her/them) at the end of a relative clause in English. In Hebrew: המלון שהתגרנו בו — the 'בו' has no equivalent in English.

exercises.question: The woman ___ called you is my colleague.
exercises.explanation: who for a person who does the action.

exercises.question: This is the book ___ I told you about.
exercises.hint: Thing as object → which or that
exercises.explanation: which or that for a thing.

exercises.question: The restaurant ___ we ate last night was fantastic.
exercises.explanation: where for a place.

exercises.question: I'll never forget the day ___ we graduated.
exercises.explanation: when for a time.

exercises.question: The man ___ car was stolen called the police.
exercises.explanation: whose = belonging to the man.

exercises.question: The people ___ work in this office are very friendly.
exercises.explanation: who for people.

exercises.question: Do you know anyone ___ speaks Mandarin?
exercises.explanation: who for a person.

exercises.question: The laptop ___ I bought last year has already broken.
exercises.explanation: which or that for a thing.

exercises.question: This is the company ___ I applied to.
exercises.explanation: which or that for organisations.

exercises.question: She is the doctor ___ treated me last year.
exercises.explanation: who for a person.

exercises.question: Can you recommend a hotel ___ has a pool?
exercises.hint: Hotel (thing) + has a pool (subject) → which or that
exercises.explanation: which or that for a thing.

exercises.question: The town ___ I was born has about 10,000 people.
exercises.explanation: where for a place.

exercises.question: The man ___ I met yesterday is a journalist.
exercises.hint: Person in object position — who or that
exercises.explanation: who (or that) for a person. Not which.

exercises.question: Is this the bag ___ you were looking for?
exercises.hint: Thing in object position → that or which
exercises.explanation: that or which for a thing.

exercises.question: She lives in the house ___ was built in 1890.
exercises.hint: House (thing) as subject of relative clause
exercises.explanation: which or that for a thing as subject.

exercises.question: Do you remember the year ___ we moved here?
exercises.explanation: when for a time period.

exercises.question: I know a man ___ brother is a famous musician.
exercises.explanation: whose = possession (it is his brother).

exercises.question: The email ___ you sent me was very helpful.
exercises.explanation: which or that for a thing. Object position — pronoun can be omitted.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'The man which helped me was very kind.'
exercises.answer: The man who helped me was very kind.
exercises.hint: which is for things. Person → who
exercises.explanation: which = things. For people use who.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'The book who I read was brilliant.'
exercises.answer: The book which/that I read was brilliant.
exercises.hint: who is for people. Book → which or that
exercises.explanation: who = people only. Things → which or that.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'This is the hotel that we stayed in it.'
exercises.answer: This is the hotel that we stayed in.
exercises.hint: Don't repeat the object with a pronoun after relative clause
exercises.explanation: In English, never repeat the noun with a pronoun. NOT 'stayed in it' — just 'stayed in'.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית: המלון שהתגרנו בו — ה'בו' לא קיים באנגלית

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'The city where I grew up it is beautiful.'
exercises.answer: The city where I grew up is beautiful.
exercises.hint: Don't add 'it' after the relative clause
exercises.explanation: The relative clause already refers back to the city. Don't repeat with 'it'.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: The woman who lives next door is a doctor.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: This is the café where I usually work in the morning.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: The man whose wallet was stolen called the police immediately.

speak.sentence: The woman who interviewed me was really professional and easy to talk to.
chunks: was really professional and easy to talk to.

speak.sentence: This is the café where I always come to work when I need to concentrate.
chunks: This is the café where I always come to work

speak.sentence: The book that changed my life was given to me by a friend.
chunks: The book that changed my life
was given to me by a friend.

speak.sentence: I still remember the day when I got the news about the job.
chunks: when I got the news about the job.

speak.sentence: The man whose car I accidentally hit was very understanding.
chunks: The man whose car I accidentally hit

speak.sentence: Do you know anyone who speaks Japanese and is looking for work?
chunks: Do you know anyone who speaks Japanese

speak.sentence: The city where I spent my childhood looks completely different now.
chunks: The city where I spent my childhood

speak.sentence: She's the colleague whose advice I always trust most.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Colleague' = KOL-eeg. 'Advice' = ad-VYS — stress on second syllable.
